mnemonics.title: Dijkstra's Key Steps
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER THE PROCESS: R-Initialize distances, E-Extract minimum, L-Relax neighbors, A-Add to solution, P-Print path. THE GREEDY CHOICE: Always pick the vertex with the SMALLEST tentative distance.

mnemonics.title: Why No Negative Weights
mnemonics.content: NEGATIVE WEIGHTS BREAK GREEDY: Dijkstra assumes once picked, a vertex is done. Negative edges can create a shorter path through an unpicked vertex, invalidating this assumption.

mnemonics.title: Complexity Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: HEAP: H-EAP HELPS EXTRACT MIN FAST - O((V+E) log V). MATRIX: MOSTLY QUADRATIC - O(V²). FIBONACCI: FASTEST - O(E + V log V).

acronyms.expansion: Shortest Path Algorithm - finds minimum distance between nodes
acronyms.usage: Dijkstra's is an SPA for single-source, non-negative weighted graphs

acronyms.expansion: Single-Source Shortest Path - one source to all vertices

acronyms.usage: Dijkstra generalizes BFS to weighted graphs

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a waiting line where the person with the smallest distance ticket is always served first. Each time we serve someone, we update all their neighbors' tickets if a shorter path is found.
Think of a seal being placed on a vertex once it's selected - it cannot be reopened. The vertex with the smallest ticket gets sealed with its final distance value.
Think of a rubber band between source and destination. If going through an intermediate vertex makes the path shorter, we 'relax' or tighten the rubber band to the shorter path.

analogies.analogy: Like a GPS finding the shortest route: it always considers the nearest unvisited location first, updates routes as it finds faster paths, and locks in the shortest route once a destination is reached.
Like constructing roads starting from city A, always extending to the closest already-connected city first. Once a city is connected, its road is final - you won't find a shorter route later.
Like water flowing downhill - it always takes the path of least resistance (shortest distance) from the source, and once it reaches a point, that path is the shortest available.
